title: Guru Namashivaya
meta_description: Guru Namasivaya was the most eminent disciple of Guhai Namasivaya who attained Self-realisation at Arunachala and was later sent to Chidambaram where he found his Guru in the form of Lord Nataraja.
body_html:
In the period of Guhai Namasivaya's life when he was living on the hill, he began to attract and teach disciples. The most eminent and well known of these disciples was a man who later became known as Guru Namasivaya.

Guru Namasivaya displayed remarkable siddhis — the ability to witness events at great distances in real time. He once laughed while witnessing a woman dancer stumble in procession at Tiruvarur, far from Tiruvannamalai; on another occasion he rubbed his cloth to prevent a curtain from burning at the Chidambaram temple. Guhai Namasivaya knew these were signs of spiritual advancement, but also knew siddhis are no real indication of progress, so he tested his disciple's level of devotion.

When Guhai Namasivaya vomited into his begging bowl and ordered his disciple to dispose of it, the disciple ate it as prasad. Seeing this, his Guru called him a true disciple and conferred the title 'Guru Namasivayamurti'.

Guhai Namasivaya then composed a venba verse comparing Guru Namasivaya to a ripe bamboo ready to be useful, and told him to go to Chidambaram. Despite the disciple's distress at leaving his Guru, he accepted and set out on foot.

On the way he composed a verse requesting food from the Goddess Unnamulai. A golden plate of sweet rice that had been forgotten in the locked Arunachaleswarar Temple was miraculously transported to him by the Goddess overnight, causing a stir in Tiruvannamalai the next morning.

On finally reaching Chidambaram and entering the main shrine for Lord Nataraja's darshan, instead of the usual dancing image, he saw the form of his beloved Guru Guhai Namasivaya. This manifestation gave him the understanding that Siva himself had manifested at Arunachala in the form of his Guru to grant him liberation:

Lord of the Golden Hall! King of Heaven! How was it that you came to dwell on holy Annamalai in the from of my Guru, [Guhai] Namasivaya, to place your twin feet upon the head of such a wretched devotee as I...

Thillai Nataraja Temple, Chidambaram

During his stay in Chidambaram he composed hundreds of verses, many of which have survived. His most famous poem, Annamalai Venba, extols Siva in the form of Arunachala and repeatedly praises the greatness of his Guru Guhai Namasivaya, whom he considered to be Arunachala-Siva in human form.

First published in The Mountain Path
notes: No hero image. Disciple of Guhai Namasivaya.
